programme.title: Miao Embroidery: History in Stitches
programme.desc: According to legend, during a migration by the Miao ethnic group, a female chieftain wanted to keep a record of all the mountains and lakes she passed along the way. The means she chose of doing so was by stitching the images on her dress. By the time they reached a suitable place to settle, her clothing was covered with embroidered designs. This, it’s said, was the origin of Miao embroidery. As the story tells us, the designs in Miao embroidery are closely related to the natural world and daily life. The Miao people have a special fondness for nature. So, as well as depicting their daily farming and work activities, they copy birds, flowers, mountains, and grass onto their clothing. Over the years, the embroidery of the Miao people has become a written record of their life. The stitches are like characters which, when many of them are combined, become a record of their history, their collected wisdom, and the knowledge passed down from their ancestors.

programme.title: Quyang Stone Carving
programme.desc: Quyang is known as a hometown of Chinese carving. Quyang's stone carving works are popular around different parts of China, thus also exported to over 100 countries and regions worldwide. The style of Quyang stone carving both carries the mysterious and rough spirit of the Wei Dynasty and the natural and graceful forms of the Tang and Song dynasties. It reflects the life experiences and live philosophies of local artisans. Each statue's showcases unique oriental aesthetics.

programme.title: An Encounter with China’s Endangered Monkeys
programme.desc: In this episode, we head to Baima Snow Mountain Nature Reserve in Diqing Tibetan Autonomous Prefecture, Yunnan Province, to learn about and participate in the conservation work for the rare and precious national treasure: the Yunnan snub-nosed monkey. The presenter ventures into the forest, experiences the daily life of local forest rangers, and witnesses the harmonious relationship between humans and monkeys. We interview \"the Monkey Father\"—Long Yongcheng, chief scientist of The Nature Conservancy (TNC) and leader of China's Primate Expert Group—who shares stories about the two decades of efforts dedicated to protecting the Yunnan snub-nosed monkey. We also go to the mountains to conduct research alongside young volunteers. Through these activities, we recognize the importance of environmental education for young people in promoting natural ecological balance and harmonious development.

programme.title: Harmony Keepers: Guardians of milu deer
programme.desc: This documentary series pays tribute to the guardians of China’s rare wildlife and their habitats. Through the lens of ecological protection and preservation, the series features four iconic protected species in China: the Tibetan antelope, milu deer, black-necked cranes, and golden snub-nosed monkeys.

programme.title: Women of the Xiangjiang River Ep.1
programme.desc: The Xiangjiang River runs 800 kilometers through the whole of Hunan Province. Hailed as the mother river of Hunan, it serves as a vital waterway connecting the Yangtze and Pearl River systems. Its source is located in Yongzhou, a land of abundant water resources, rich natural treasures and outstanding people. In this episode, we focus on three young women from Yongzhou: Pan Haiying, a dance teacher; Huang Cuilan, an inheritor of brocade weaving; and Hu Xin, a custodian of Nüshu culture. We document snippets of their daily lives and work, to reflect the profound cultural heritage along the Xiangjiang River and the value pursuits of the women living on its banks.

programme.title: Women of the Xiangjiang River Ep.2
programme.desc: In this episode, we continue our journey along the Xiangjiang River in Hunan Province, to meet more outstanding women from the cities of Hengyang and Zhuzhou. By documenting snippets of the daily lives and work of a paper-cutting artist, the director of the Hengyang Huagu Opera Troupe, and an inheritor of Liling porcelain craftsmanship, we gain vivid insights into the distinctive cultural traditions of this region.

programme.title: Women of the Xiangjiang River Ep.3
programme.desc: In this episode, we continue our journey along the Xiangjiang River in Hunan Province, traveling to the cities of Zhuzhou and Xiangtan to meet another group of extraordinary women. By capturing snippets of the lives and work of an electric locomotive designer, a flight instructor, a painter, and a poverty alleviation volunteer, we showcase the unique cultural and humanistic charm of this region.

programme.title: Ancient tradition in an old town takes on new life
programme.desc: The Chinese lion dance is an art form that combines acrobatics, dance, folk music and embroidery. The southern style lion dance was a staple in the ancient town of Qiantong before the costume faded in the early 20th century. However, now the tradition has been revived by some incredible talented young people from this town.

programme.title: Ningxia: Echoes of the Yellow River
programme.desc: Ningxia: Echoes of the Yellow River is a documentary focusing on the World Heritage sites and cultural–ecological conservation in the Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region. Through the lens of Ningxia’s heritage stories, the film highlights China’s commitment to World Heritage preservation, ecological restoration, and cultural continuity — presenting to global audiences a land where history and modernity intertwine, and where desert and oasis coexist.\nBy following the guardians of the Western Xia Imperial Tombs, desert restorers in the Tengger Desert, Great Wall rangers at Sanguankou, traditional sheepskin raft craftsmen, and managers of the Yellow River irrigation system in Qingtongxia, the program reveals Ningxia’s distinctive contributions to cultural heritage, natural ecology, and intangible traditions — and the enduring human spirit behind them.

programme.title: A Lifelong Journey of Xuan Paper
programme.desc: The host travels to Jingxian, Anhui—known as the hometown of Xuan paper—to experience the intricate process of making this traditional paper. Along the way, they uncover the secrets behind its thousand-year legacy and witness the craftsmanship of Xuan paper inheritors at every stage. Through vivid, personal stories, the episode reveals the hidden struggles and quiet perseverance that have kept this intangible cultural heritage alive across generations.

programme.title: Awakening Lion
programme.desc: Xingshi, or literally “awakened lion,” is a name for the southern style of the lion dance. Historically, it evolved from a court dance in the Tang Dynasty almost 1,200 years ago. With the southward migration of the population from the central plains, lion dance culture was introduced to the south. In the Ming Dynasty around 400 years ago, xingshi appeared in Guangdong and was then widely spread throughout southern China and even overseas. Lion dance has become a very important part of Chinese culture.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS Inheritance matters more than fame
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: Miao Embroidery: History in Stitches
programme.desc: According to legend, during a migration by the Miao ethnic group, a female chieftain wanted to keep a record of all the mountains and lakes she passed along the way. The means she chose of doing so was by stitching the images on her dress. By the time they reached a suitable place to settle, her clothing was covered with embroidered designs. This, it’s said, was the origin of Miao embroidery. As the story tells us, the designs in Miao embroidery are closely related to the natural world and daily life. The Miao people have a special fondness for nature. So, as well as depicting their daily farming and work activities, they copy birds, flowers, mountains, and grass onto their clothing. Over the years, the embroidery of the Miao people has become a written record of their life. The stitches are like characters which, when many of them are combined, become a record of their history, their collected wisdom, and the knowledge passed down from their ancestors.

programme.title: Quyang Stone Carving
programme.desc: Quyang is known as a hometown of Chinese carving. Quyang's stone carving works are popular around different parts of China, thus also exported to over 100 countries and regions worldwide. The style of Quyang stone carving both carries the mysterious and rough spirit of the Wei Dynasty and the natural and graceful forms of the Tang and Song dynasties. It reflects the life experiences and live philosophies of local artisans. Each statue's showcases unique oriental aesthetics.

programme.title: An Encounter with China’s Endangered Monkeys
programme.desc: In this episode, we head to Baima Snow Mountain Nature Reserve in Diqing Tibetan Autonomous Prefecture, Yunnan Province, to learn about and participate in the conservation work for the rare and precious national treasure: the Yunnan snub-nosed monkey. The presenter ventures into the forest, experiences the daily life of local forest rangers, and witnesses the harmonious relationship between humans and monkeys. We interview \"the Monkey Father\"—Long Yongcheng, chief scientist of The Nature Conservancy (TNC) and leader of China's Primate Expert Group—who shares stories about the two decades of efforts dedicated to protecting the Yunnan snub-nosed monkey. We also go to the mountains to conduct research alongside young volunteers. Through these activities, we recognize the importance of environmental education for young people in promoting natural ecological balance and harmonious development.

programme.title: Harmony Keepers: Guardians of milu deer
programme.desc: This documentary series pays tribute to the guardians of China’s rare wildlife and their habitats. Through the lens of ecological protection and preservation, the series features four iconic protected species in China: the Tibetan antelope, milu deer, black-necked cranes, and golden snub-nosed monkeys.

programme.title: Women of the Xiangjiang River Ep.1
programme.desc: The Xiangjiang River runs 800 kilometers through the whole of Hunan Province. Hailed as the mother river of Hunan, it serves as a vital waterway connecting the Yangtze and Pearl River systems. Its source is located in Yongzhou, a land of abundant water resources, rich natural treasures and outstanding people. In this episode, we focus on three young women from Yongzhou: Pan Haiying, a dance teacher; Huang Cuilan, an inheritor of brocade weaving; and Hu Xin, a custodian of Nüshu culture. We document snippets of their daily lives and work, to reflect the profound cultural heritage along the Xiangjiang River and the value pursuits of the women living on its banks.

programme.title: Women of the Xiangjiang River Ep.2
programme.desc: In this episode, we continue our journey along the Xiangjiang River in Hunan Province, to meet more outstanding women from the cities of Hengyang and Zhuzhou. By documenting snippets of the daily lives and work of a paper-cutting artist, the director of the Hengyang Huagu Opera Troupe, and an inheritor of Liling porcelain craftsmanship, we gain vivid insights into the distinctive cultural traditions of this region.

programme.title: Women of the Xiangjiang River Ep.3
programme.desc: In this episode, we continue our journey along the Xiangjiang River in Hunan Province, traveling to the cities of Zhuzhou and Xiangtan to meet another group of extraordinary women. By capturing snippets of the lives and work of an electric locomotive designer, a flight instructor, a painter, and a poverty alleviation volunteer, we showcase the unique cultural and humanistic charm of this region.

programme.title: Ancient tradition in an old town takes on new life
programme.desc: The Chinese lion dance is an art form that combines acrobatics, dance, folk music and embroidery. The southern style lion dance was a staple in the ancient town of Qiantong before the costume faded in the early 20th century. However, now the tradition has been revived by some incredible talented young people from this town.

programme.title: Ningxia: Echoes of the Yellow River
programme.desc: Ningxia: Echoes of the Yellow River is a documentary focusing on the World Heritage sites and cultural–ecological conservation in the Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region. Through the lens of Ningxia’s heritage stories, the film highlights China’s commitment to World Heritage preservation, ecological restoration, and cultural continuity — presenting to global audiences a land where history and modernity intertwine, and where desert and oasis coexist.\nBy following the guardians of the Western Xia Imperial Tombs, desert restorers in the Tengger Desert, Great Wall rangers at Sanguankou, traditional sheepskin raft craftsmen, and managers of the Yellow River irrigation system in Qingtongxia, the program reveals Ningxia’s distinctive contributions to cultural heritage, natural ecology, and intangible traditions — and the enduring human spirit behind them.

programme.title: A Lifelong Journey of Xuan Paper
programme.desc: The host travels to Jingxian, Anhui—known as the hometown of Xuan paper—to experience the intricate process of making this traditional paper. Along the way, they uncover the secrets behind its thousand-year legacy and witness the craftsmanship of Xuan paper inheritors at every stage. Through vivid, personal stories, the episode reveals the hidden struggles and quiet perseverance that have kept this intangible cultural heritage alive across generations.

programme.title: Awakening Lion
programme.desc: Xingshi, or literally “awakened lion,” is a name for the southern style of the lion dance. Historically, it evolved from a court dance in the Tang Dynasty almost 1,200 years ago. With the southward migration of the population from the central plains, lion dance culture was introduced to the south. In the Ming Dynasty around 400 years ago, xingshi appeared in Guangdong and was then widely spread throughout southern China and even overseas. Lion dance has become a very important part of Chinese culture.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS Inheritance matters more than fame
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: Miao Embroidery: History in Stitches
programme.desc: According to legend, during a migration by the Miao ethnic group, a female chieftain wanted to keep a record of all the mountains and lakes she passed along the way. The means she chose of doing so was by stitching the images on her dress. By the time they reached a suitable place to settle, her clothing was covered with embroidered designs. This, it’s said, was the origin of Miao embroidery. As the story tells us, the designs in Miao embroidery are closely related to the natural world and daily life. The Miao people have a special fondness for nature. So, as well as depicting their daily farming and work activities, they copy birds, flowers, mountains, and grass onto their clothing. Over the years, the embroidery of the Miao people has become a written record of their life. The stitches are like characters which, when many of them are combined, become a record of their history, their collected wisdom, and the knowledge passed down from their ancestors.

programme.title: Quyang Stone Carving
programme.desc: Quyang is known as a hometown of Chinese carving. Quyang's stone carving works are popular around different parts of China, thus also exported to over 100 countries and regions worldwide. The style of Quyang stone carving both carries the mysterious and rough spirit of the Wei Dynasty and the natural and graceful forms of the Tang and Song dynasties. It reflects the life experiences and live philosophies of local artisans. Each statue's showcases unique oriental aesthetics.

programme.title: Women of the Xiangjiang River Ep.4
programme.desc: In this episode, we arrive in Changsha — the economic and cultural hub of Hunan Province, and a core city in the Yangtze River Middle Reaches Economic Zone. Our lens turns to the \"Xiangjiang Ladies\" who work in Changsha’s high-tech and equipment manufacturing sectors. Their daily lives and professional endeavors reflect the city’s innovative vitality, while their after-work gatherings also showcase the dazzling night views along both banks of the Xiangjiang River, highlighting Changsha’s distinct charm as a vibrant capital of leisure and entertainment.

programme.title: The Art of Harmony: Human Becoming
programme.desc: “The Art of Harmony” is a documentary about Confucianism, produced by CGTN and Discovery Channel. It highlights the timeless nature of the sage’s philosophy and its global relevance today. Join us in exploring how the world might benefit from the pursuit of “The Art of Harmony”.

programme.title: All About the Yak
programme.desc: In this episode, we go to Nangqian, Yushu, Qinghai Province, to get close contact with the Yak in Tibet, which is known as the \" Vehicle on the Plateau\". During the bustling yak culture festival, people gather from near and far, meadows are crowded, and yak appraisal activities are also in full swing. As the journey continues, we are able to understand several aspects of the yak culture which is closely related to the life of Tibetan people.

programme.title: The Yak and the Nomad
programme.desc: Qinghai’s Yushu Prefecture is one of China’s key yak-producing regions. This trip took us to Nangqian County, where we explored the yak industry that lies at the heart of Tibetan herders’ daily lives. Trekking into the far-flung pastoral corners of the plateau, we listened closely to the stories behind the hurdles and hopes of pastoral life and development.

programme.title: China Bound: Tianjin
programme.desc: Tianran He explores the vibrant port city of Tianjin. Strolling through its historic quarters, he discovers how old buildings are being preserved and revitalized, and savors the rich flavors of authentic Tianjin cuisine. To work off the calories, he joins energetic retirees for a morning workout in the park, then takes in the lively nightlife along the banks of the Haihe River. From folk handicrafts on Ancient Culture Street to the National Maritime Museum and an aircraft carrier theme park, Tianran discovers that Tianjin is a truly multifacted city.

programme.title: Young star on green field
programme.desc: Meet Sun Chenxi, a 12-year-old soccer \"star\" from Ningbo who first picked up the game at the age of four and is already playing with the confidence of a veteran. Sun says he dreams of turning pro one day and leading China's men's team to the World Cup. Dribbling and passing with skills far more advanced than his age, he has won over audiences far and wide after his father posted clips of Sun flashing his fancy footwork on social media. The soon-to-be-teenager now has millions of followers online. Already recognized as a rising young talent, high hopes are pinned on Sun as he is cheered on by his parents, friends, and throngs of fans online.

programme.title: Celadon Ou Music
programme.desc: Celadon ou music is also called \"bowl music.” The reason is that the ancient Chinese word for bowl sounds like ou. The music originated simply as improvised rhythms tapped out on bowls. It was this spontaneity that made celadon ou so popular. Celadon ou music was at the height of its popularity over 1,100 years ago. But with time, it faded from history. At one point, it was even on the verge of being lost forever. After hundreds of years of obscurity, it re-emerged at the end of last century.

programme.title: Kesi
programme.desc: Silk is a classic symbol of Chinese culture. It helped opened up economic and cultural exchanges between East and West and witnessed the spread of Eastern civilization to the world. As a representative of traditional Chinese silk art, kesi is renowned for its highly ornamental weaving techniques of highlighting warps and breaking wefts, and has been deeply loved by people for thousands of years. In folk culture, it enjoys the reputation of “an inch of kesi is worth an inch of gold.

programme.title: A Lifelong Journey of Xuan Paper
programme.desc: The host travels to Jingxian, Anhui—known as the hometown of Xuan paper—to experience the intricate process of making this traditional paper. Along the way, they uncover the secrets behind its thousand-year legacy and witness the craftsmanship of Xuan paper inheritors at every stage. Through vivid, personal stories, the episode reveals the hidden struggles and quiet perseverance that have kept this intangible cultural heritage alive across generations.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS Inheritance matters more than fame
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: Miao Silverware: A Shining Epic
programme.desc: In this episode, we explore the fascinating world of the silver-working art of the Miao ethnic group. Join us, and hear the stories and see the works of four remarkable individuals. From a national-level inheritor who is preserving the traditional craft, to an artist infusing personal flair into his designs; from a female silversmith imbuing her work with feminine warmth, to a young artisan inspiring a new generation, we investigate the rich heritage, cultural significance, and evolving potential of Miao silverware.

programme.title: Xinjiang On the Road
programme.desc: Join an international group on an ice-snow adventure across northern Xinjiang, exploring stunning winter scenery, rich ethnic culture and warm hospitality from Urumqi to Altay.

programme.title: Women of the Xiangjiang River Ep.4
programme.desc: In this episode, we arrive in Changsha — the economic and cultural hub of Hunan Province, and a core city in the Yangtze River Middle Reaches Economic Zone. Our lens turns to the \"Xiangjiang Ladies\" who work in Changsha’s high-tech and equipment manufacturing sectors. Their daily lives and professional endeavors reflect the city’s innovative vitality, while their after-work gatherings also showcase the dazzling night views along both banks of the Xiangjiang River, highlighting Changsha’s distinct charm as a vibrant capital of leisure and entertainment.

programme.title: The Art of Harmony: Human Becoming
programme.desc: “The Art of Harmony” is a documentary about Confucianism, produced by CGTN and Discovery Channel. It highlights the timeless nature of the sage’s philosophy and its global relevance today. Join us in exploring how the world might benefit from the pursuit of “The Art of Harmony”.

programme.title: All About the Yak
programme.desc: In this episode, we go to Nangqian, Yushu, Qinghai Province, to get close contact with the Yak in Tibet, which is known as the \" Vehicle on the Plateau\". During the bustling yak culture festival, people gather from near and far, meadows are crowded, and yak appraisal activities are also in full swing. As the journey continues, we are able to understand several aspects of the yak culture which is closely related to the life of Tibetan people.

programme.title: The Yak and the Nomad
programme.desc: Qinghai’s Yushu Prefecture is one of China’s key yak-producing regions. This trip took us to Nangqian County, where we explored the yak industry that lies at the heart of Tibetan herders’ daily lives. Trekking into the far-flung pastoral corners of the plateau, we listened closely to the stories behind the hurdles and hopes of pastoral life and development.

programme.title: China Bound: Tianjin
programme.desc: Tianran He explores the vibrant port city of Tianjin. Strolling through its historic quarters, he discovers how old buildings are being preserved and revitalized, and savors the rich flavors of authentic Tianjin cuisine. To work off the calories, he joins energetic retirees for a morning workout in the park, then takes in the lively nightlife along the banks of the Haihe River. From folk handicrafts on Ancient Culture Street to the National Maritime Museum and an aircraft carrier theme park, Tianran discovers that Tianjin is a truly multifacted city.

programme.title: Young star on green field
programme.desc: Meet Sun Chenxi, a 12-year-old soccer \"star\" from Ningbo who first picked up the game at the age of four and is already playing with the confidence of a veteran. Sun says he dreams of turning pro one day and leading China's men's team to the World Cup. Dribbling and passing with skills far more advanced than his age, he has won over audiences far and wide after his father posted clips of Sun flashing his fancy footwork on social media. The soon-to-be-teenager now has millions of followers online. Already recognized as a rising young talent, high hopes are pinned on Sun as he is cheered on by his parents, friends, and throngs of fans online.

programme.title: Celadon Ou Music
programme.desc: Celadon ou music is also called \"bowl music.” The reason is that the ancient Chinese word for bowl sounds like ou. The music originated simply as improvised rhythms tapped out on bowls. It was this spontaneity that made celadon ou so popular. Celadon ou music was at the height of its popularity over 1,100 years ago. But with time, it faded from history. At one point, it was even on the verge of being lost forever. After hundreds of years of obscurity, it re-emerged at the end of last century.

programme.title: Kesi
programme.desc: Silk is a classic symbol of Chinese culture. It helped opened up economic and cultural exchanges between East and West and witnessed the spread of Eastern civilization to the world. As a representative of traditional Chinese silk art, kesi is renowned for its highly ornamental weaving techniques of highlighting warps and breaking wefts, and has been deeply loved by people for thousands of years. In folk culture, it enjoys the reputation of “an inch of kesi is worth an inch of gold.

programme.title: A Lifelong Journey of Xuan Paper
programme.desc: The host travels to Jingxian, Anhui—known as the hometown of Xuan paper—to experience the intricate process of making this traditional paper. Along the way, they uncover the secrets behind its thousand-year legacy and witness the craftsmanship of Xuan paper inheritors at every stage. Through vivid, personal stories, the episode reveals the hidden struggles and quiet perseverance that have kept this intangible cultural heritage alive across generations.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS Inheritance matters more than fame
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: Miao Silverware: A Shining Epic
programme.desc: In this episode, we explore the fascinating world of the silver-working art of the Miao ethnic group. Join us, and hear the stories and see the works of four remarkable individuals. From a national-level inheritor who is preserving the traditional craft, to an artist infusing personal flair into his designs; from a female silversmith imbuing her work with feminine warmth, to a young artisan inspiring a new generation, we investigate the rich heritage, cultural significance, and evolving potential of Miao silverware.

programme.desc: Join an international group on an ice-snow adventure across northern Xinjiang, exploring stunning winter scenery, rich ethnic culture and warm hospitality from Urumqi to Altay.

programme.desc: In this episode, we arrive in Changsha — the economic and cultural hub of Hunan Province, and a core city in the Yangtze River Middle Reaches Economic Zone. Our lens turns to the \"Xiangjiang Ladies\" who work in Changsha’s high-tech and equipment manufacturing sectors. Their daily lives and professional endeavors reflect the city’s innovative vitality, while their after-work gatherings also showcase the dazzling night views along both banks of the Xiangjiang River, highlighting Changsha’s distinct charm as a vibrant capital of leisure and entertainment.

programme.desc: “The Art of Harmony” is a documentary about Confucianism, produced by CGTN and Discovery Channel. It highlights the timeless nature of the sage’s philosophy and its global relevance today. Join us in exploring how the world might benefit from the pursuit of “The Art of Harmony”.

As the journey continues, we are able to understand several aspects of the yak culture which is closely related to the life of Tibetan people.

programme.desc: Qinghai’s Yushu Prefecture is one of China’s key yak-producing regions. This trip took us to Nangqian County, where we explored the yak industry that lies at the heart of Tibetan herders’ daily lives. Trekking into the far-flung pastoral corners of the plateau, we listened closely to the stories behind the hurdles and hopes of pastoral life and development.

Strolling through its historic quarters, he discovers how old buildings are being preserved and revitalized, and savors the rich flavors of authentic Tianjin cuisine. To work off the calories, he joins energetic retirees for a morning workout in the park, then takes in the lively nightlife along the banks of the Haihe River. The music originated simply as improvised rhythms tapped out on bowls. It was this spontaneity that made celadon ou so popular. Celadon ou music was at the height of its popularity over 1,100 years ago. But with time, it faded from history. At one point, it was even on the verge of being lost forever. After hundreds of years of obscurity, it re-emerged at the end of last century.

programme.desc: Silk is a classic symbol of Chinese culture. It helped opened up economic and cultural exchanges between East and West and witnessed the spread of Eastern civilization to the world. As a representative of traditional Chinese silk art, kesi is renowned for its highly ornamental weaving techniques of highlighting warps and breaking wefts, and has been deeply loved by people for thousands of years. In folk culture, it enjoys the reputation of “an inch of kesi is worth an inch of gold.

programme.desc: The host travels to Jingxian, Anhui—known as the hometown of Xuan paper—to experience the intricate process of making this traditional paper. Along the way, they uncover the secrets behind its thousand-year legacy and witness the craftsmanship of Xuan paper inheritors at every stage. Through vivid, personal stories, the episode reveals the hidden struggles and quiet perseverance that have kept this intangible cultural heritage alive across generations.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS Inheritance matters more than fame
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: Miao Silverware: A Shining Epic
programme.desc: In this episode, we explore the fascinating world of the silver-working art of the Miao ethnic group. Join us, and hear the stories and see the works of four remarkable individuals. From a national-level inheritor who is preserving the traditional craft, to an artist infusing personal flair into his designs; from a female silversmith imbuing her work with feminine warmth, to a young artisan inspiring a new generation, we investigate the rich heritage, cultural significance, and evolving potential of Miao silverware.

programme.title: Xinjiang On the Road
programme.desc: Join an international group on an ice-snow adventure across northern Xinjiang, exploring stunning winter scenery, rich ethnic culture and warm hospitality from Urumqi to Altay.

programme.title: Women of the Xiangjiang River Ep.4
programme.desc: In this episode, we arrive in Changsha — the economic and cultural hub of Hunan Province, and a core city in the Yangtze River Middle Reaches Economic Zone. Our lens turns to the \"Xiangjiang Ladies\" who work in Changsha’s high-tech and equipment manufacturing sectors. Their daily lives and professional endeavors reflect the city’s innovative vitality, while their after-work gatherings also showcase the dazzling night views along both banks of the Xiangjiang River, highlighting Changsha’s distinct charm as a vibrant capital of leisure and entertainment.

programme.title: The Art of Harmony: Human Becoming
programme.desc: “The Art of Harmony” is a documentary about Confucianism, produced by CGTN and Discovery Channel. It highlights the timeless nature of the sage’s philosophy and its global relevance today. Join us in exploring how the world might benefit from the pursuit of “The Art of Harmony”.

programme.title: All About the Yak
programme.desc: In this episode, we go to Nangqian, Yushu, Qinghai Province, to get close contact with the Yak in Tibet, which is known as the \" Vehicle on the Plateau\". During the bustling yak culture festival, people gather from near and far, meadows are crowded, and yak appraisal activities are also in full swing. As the journey continues, we are able to understand several aspects of the yak culture which is closely related to the life of Tibetan people.

programme.title: The Yak and the Nomad
programme.desc: Qinghai’s Yushu Prefecture is one of China’s key yak-producing regions. This trip took us to Nangqian County, where we explored the yak industry that lies at the heart of Tibetan herders’ daily lives. Trekking into the far-flung pastoral corners of the plateau, we listened closely to the stories behind the hurdles and hopes of pastoral life and development.

programme.title: China Bound: Tianjin
programme.desc: Tianran He explores the vibrant port city of Tianjin. Strolling through its historic quarters, he discovers how old buildings are being preserved and revitalized, and savors the rich flavors of authentic Tianjin cuisine. To work off the calories, he joins energetic retirees for a morning workout in the park, then takes in the lively nightlife along the banks of the Haihe River. From folk handicrafts on Ancient Culture Street to the National Maritime Museum and an aircraft carrier theme park, Tianran discovers that Tianjin is a truly multifacted city.

programme.title: Young star on green field
programme.desc: Meet Sun Chenxi, a 12-year-old soccer \"star\" from Ningbo who first picked up the game at the age of four and is already playing with the confidence of a veteran. Sun says he dreams of turning pro one day and leading China's men's team to the World Cup. Dribbling and passing with skills far more advanced than his age, he has won over audiences far and wide after his father posted clips of Sun flashing his fancy footwork on social media. The soon-to-be-teenager now has millions of followers online. Already recognized as a rising young talent, high hopes are pinned on Sun as he is cheered on by his parents, friends, and throngs of fans online.

programme.title: Celadon Ou Music
programme.desc: Celadon ou music is also called \"bowl music.” The reason is that the ancient Chinese word for bowl sounds like ou. The music originated simply as improvised rhythms tapped out on bowls. It was this spontaneity that made celadon ou so popular. Celadon ou music was at the height of its popularity over 1,100 years ago. But with time, it faded from history. At one point, it was even on the verge of being lost forever. After hundreds of years of obscurity, it re-emerged at the end of last century.

programme.title: Kesi
programme.desc: Silk is a classic symbol of Chinese culture. It helped opened up economic and cultural exchanges between East and West and witnessed the spread of Eastern civilization to the world. As a representative of traditional Chinese silk art, kesi is renowned for its highly ornamental weaving techniques of highlighting warps and breaking wefts, and has been deeply loved by people for thousands of years. In folk culture, it enjoys the reputation of “an inch of kesi is worth an inch of gold.

programme.title: A Lifelong Journey of Xuan Paper
programme.desc: The host travels to Jingxian, Anhui—known as the hometown of Xuan paper—to experience the intricate process of making this traditional paper. Along the way, they uncover the secrets behind its thousand-year legacy and witness the craftsmanship of Xuan paper inheritors at every stage. Through vivid, personal stories, the episode reveals the hidden struggles and quiet perseverance that have kept this intangible cultural heritage alive across generations.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS Inheritance matters more than fame
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: Miao Silverware: A Shining Epic
programme.desc: In this episode, we explore the fascinating world of the silver-working art of the Miao ethnic group. Join us, and hear the stories and see the works of four remarkable individuals. From a national-level inheritor who is preserving the traditional craft, to an artist infusing personal flair into his designs; from a female silversmith imbuing her work with feminine warmth, to a young artisan inspiring a new generation, we investigate the rich heritage, cultural significance, and evolving potential of Miao silverware.

programme.title: Xinjiang On the Road
programme.desc: Join an international group on an ice-snow adventure across northern Xinjiang, exploring stunning winter scenery, rich ethnic culture and warm hospitality from Urumqi to Altay.

programme.title: China Untapped: Nanjing 1
programme.desc: Nanjing, an ancient city that served as the capital of six imperial dynasties, stands as a testament to China's long-standing history and rich culture. From its Porcelain Tower - one of the Seven Wonders of the Middle Ages, to its grand city walls and ancient palaces, join us on a voyage across time to experience the echoes of history in Nanjing's storied bricks.

programme.title: The Art of Harmony: The Rite Society
programme.desc: “The Art of Harmony” is a documentary about Confucianism, produced by CGTN and Discovery Channel. It highlights the timeless nature of the sage’s philosophy and its global relevance today. Join us in exploring how the world might benefit from the pursuit of “The Art of Harmony”.

programme.title: China Untapped: Nanjing 2
programme.desc: In this episode, we explore the grand old mansions of Nanjing’s merchant families and take a stroll through their exquisite gardens, where Nature is incorporated into the architecture. From centuries-old Qing Dynasty residences to traditional folk handicrafts, join us on a deep dive into Nanjing’s rich cultural heritage.

programme.title: China Untapped: Nanjing 3
programme.desc: Join us in Nanjing’s bustling old quarter, where you can try on silk dresses worth their weight in gold, and see how traditional handicrafts have been given modern twists. We’ll feast on street snacks and local delicacies by the old Confucian Temple, before taking a pleasure cruise along Nanjing’s mother river to round off our exploration of this vibrant and historic city.

programme.title: Nyingchi Ep.1
programme.desc: Join Tianran He in Nyingchi, Xizang Autonomous Region. Fed by the Yarlung Zangbo River and abundant rainfall, this lush subtropical region is home to some of Xizan’g most unique scenery. From sacred mountains and ancient monasteries, to cruising along Nyingchi’s mother river, wandering alpine meadows and savoring stone pot chicken – a beloved local delicacy, Tianran uncovers the beauty of Nyingchi.

programme.title: Basketball dreams soar in Liangshan
programme.desc: Meet basketball girl Aguolieha, who comes from a rural village in the mountainous Liangshan Yi Autonomous Prefecture in southwest China. Playing for a local team called the Blackhawks, the 13-year-old has a big dream: to become a professional player one day and join Team China. At first, her father was not supportive as he feared that sports training would make her grades fall. Proving to be excellent both academically and on the court, she often looks up to her idol Stephen Curry, and trains hard with her peers. She believes that the sky's the limit and that basketball games will help her dreams soar.

programme.title: The sound of casting
programme.desc: For centuries, the fires of craftsmanship have never faded in Pingyao. In this UNESCO World Heritage city once famed for trade, three ancient metalworking traditions — sword casting, gold and silver crafting, and iron forging — continue to thrive. This episode steps into the workshops where artisans shape metal with fire and devotion. Each blade’s edge, each ornament’s gleam, and each curve of wrought iron tells a story of resilience and artistry — revealing how the spirit of Pingyao’s craftsmanship still glows in the heart of time.

programme.title: Indigo Dreams: A Hue of Dali Tie-dye
programme.desc: Dali Tie-dye is the unique textile art of the Bai people, renowned for its exquisite hand-tying and traditional indigo dyeing techniques that create brilliant, one-of-a-kind patterns. Explore the cultural heritage and enduring beauty of this ancient craft from Yunnan.

programme.title: Grand Song of the Dong — Natural Oriental Harmony
programme.desc:
The Grand Song of the Dong is a polyphonic folk singing tradition of the Dong ethnic group. Performers from several villages gather together to sing, unaccompanied.
The songs, which describe the history, beliefs, customs, and emotions of the Dong people, are an important part of their cultural identity and contribute significantly to their social cohesion.
Generally sung in the Dong language, which has no written form, they are passed on orally from one generation to the next. Classified into various genres—including narrative and love songs, drinking and funeral songs—they are often accompanied by clapping, stomping, and dancing.
Sung on occasions such as festivals, weddings, and funerals, the Grand Song, as a valuable part of the Dong ethnic group’s cultural heritage, reflects their wisdom and creativity, as well as their harmony with nature.

programme.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS Ancient Ru Kiln embraces its new era
programme.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programme.title: Exploring Ethnic Attire: Dong Elegance
programme.desc: Dong clothing weaves together tradition, craftsmanship, and the unique identity of the Dong ethnic group. In this episode, we’ll unravel the stories behind the intricate designs, vibrant colors, and deep cultural significance of Dong attire. From ceremonial garments to everyday wear, each piece is imbued with the essence of a rich heritage.

programme.title: Fujian’s Tulou – The Mysterious Earthen Castles
programme.desc: The tulous, or earth-built dwellings, of Fujian are recognized as treasures of traditional architecture, thanks to their unique design, cultural importance, and embodiment of the concept of harmonious coexistence with nature.\nTulous are mostly rectangular or circular in form, with a simple, elegant exterior and a well-furnished interior. As homes built by the Hakka people, they are imbued with diligence, wisdom, and the pursuit of excellence. Surrounded by mountains and waters, the tulous blend well with the natural environment. When building their tulous, the Hakka people pay attention to ecological balance in order to minimize their environmental footprint.\nA tulou is not only a safe place to live, but also the crystallization of the culture and architectural skill passed down for generations among the Hakka people.

programme.title: China Untapped: Nanjing 1
programme.desc: Nanjing, an ancient city that served as the capital of six imperial dynasties, stands as a testament to China's long-standing history and rich culture. From its Porcelain Tower - one of the Seven Wonders of the Middle Ages, to its grand city walls and ancient palaces, join us on a voyage across time to experience the echoes of history in Nanjing's storied bricks.

programme.title: The Art of Harmony: The Rite Society
programme.desc: “The Art of Harmony” is a documentary about Confucianism, produced by CGTN and Discovery Channel. It highlights the timeless nature of the sage’s philosophy and its global relevance today. Join us in exploring how the world might benefit from the pursuit of “The Art of Harmony”.

programme.title: China Untapped: Nanjing 2
programme.desc: In this episode, we explore the grand old mansions of Nanjing’s merchant families and take a stroll through their exquisite gardens, where Nature is incorporated into the architecture. From centuries-old Qing Dynasty residences to traditional folk handicrafts, join us on a deep dive into Nanjing’s rich cultural heritage.

programme.title: China Untapped: Nanjing 3
programme.desc: Join us in Nanjing’s bustling old quarter, where you can try on silk dresses worth their weight in gold, and see how traditional handicrafts have been given modern twists. We’ll feast on street snacks and local delicacies by the old Confucian Temple, before taking a pleasure cruise along Nanjing’s mother river to round off our exploration of this vibrant and historic city.
